%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 5.7 Digital Processing of Analog Signals

> **Section Objective:** Design a discrete-time processor H[z] so a sampled-and-reconstructed digital chain behaves like a desired analog LTIC system Ha(s).

---

## Concepts In This Section

- C/D and D/C processing chain
- Sample-level equivalence
- Analog output samples
- Discrete-time convolution
- Impulse invariance


## 1. The Digital Chain Is Trying to Imitate an Analog System

An analog input \(x(t)\) is first sampled by a C/D converter into a discrete sequence \(x[n]\), then processed by a discrete-time system \(H[z]\), and finally converted back into an analog signal \(y(t)\) by a D/C converter.

The desired analog system \(H_a(s)\) would map \(x(t)\) directly to \(y(t)\) without any sampling. The digital route takes a detour through the discrete domain.

> **The design target is not just to make a digital filter; it is to make the digital route imitate the analog route at the sample instants.**

For example, if the analog system output sample at \(t = 7T\) is \(0.42\), the digital output \(y[7]\) should also be \(0.42\).

#### Note
Aliasing and frequency-domain design are separate concerns — this section focuses on the time-domain matching criterion.

## 2. Equivalence Means Matching Output Samples

The digital implementation is considered **equivalent** to the analog target when every discrete output sample \(y[n]\) equals the analog-system output observed at time \(t = nT\).

- \(n\) is the sample index (an integer).
- \(T\) is the sampling period in seconds.
- \(nT\) is the specific analog time instant corresponding to sample \(n\).

### WHEN TO USE THIS
Whenever an exam asks for a digital processor that **realizes or approximates** an analog LTIC system, this is the equivalence condition to invoke.

### COMMON MISUSE
Assuming the reconstructed analog waveform must equal the analog target at **every** time \(t\) — that is a stronger condition that depends on reconstruction assumptions beyond this criterion.

> **Near-miss:** Wrong: \(y[n] = y(t)\). Correct: \(y[n] = y(nT)\).

$$y[n] = y(nT)$$

## 3. Sample the Analog Convolution Output — Equation (5.42)

This formula gives the **output samples of the desired analog LTIC system** when the analog input is sampled at \(t = mT\).

**Symbol definitions:**
- \(x(mT)\) — the analog input evaluated at sample instant \(t = mT\)
- \(h_a(t)\) — the analog impulse response of the target system
- \(h_a[(n-m)T]\) — the analog impulse response evaluated at time \((n-m)T\)
- \(T\) — the sampling period; the leading \(T\) arises from relating the continuous-time convolution integral to a sample-indexed sum
- \(m, n\) — integer summation and output indices

### EXAM TRIGGER
Use equation (5.42) when the problem starts from the analog impulse response \(h_a(t)\) and asks what output samples should be matched.

### COMMON MISUSE
Dropping \(T\), or replacing \(h_a[(n-m)T]\) with \(h_a[n-m]\) as if \(h_a\) were already a discrete-time sequence.

> **Example:** At \(n = 3\), the impulse response is evaluated at \(\ldots, 2T, T, 0, -T, \ldots\) — not at abstract integer points.

$$y(nT) = T\sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x(mT)\, h_a[(n-m)T]$$

## 4. The Digital Processor Obeys Ordinary Discrete-Time Convolution — Equation (5.43)

This is the output of the digital filter \(H[z]\) with impulse response \(h[n]\).

**Symbol definitions:**
- \(x[m]\) — the input sequence at index \(m\)
- \(h[n-m]\) — the digital impulse response shifted to index \(n-m\)
- \(m, n\) — integer summation and output indices

This formula has **no leading \(T\)** because it is a native discrete-time convolution sum operating directly on sequences.

### EXAM TRIGGER
Use equation (5.43) when \(H[z]\) or \(h[n]\) is the digital processor being designed or analyzed.

### COMMON MISUSE
Adding an extra \(T\) here just because equation (5.42) has one — the \(T\) belongs to the analog-sampled expression, not to the digital filter's own computation.

> **Comparison:** Equation (5.42) describes the analog target sampled in time; equation (5.43) describes the digital filter's native computation.

$$y[n] = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\, h[n-m]$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="comparison_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 5. Impulse Invariance Criterion — Equation (5.44)

Matching equation (5.42) to equation (5.43) term by term yields the design rule for the digital filter impulse response.

**Symbol definitions:**
- \(h[n]\) — the discrete-time impulse response of the digital processor \(H[z]\)
- \(h_a(t)\) — the analog impulse response of the target system \(H_a(s)\)
- \(T\) — the sampling period
- \(nT\) — the analog time instant at which the sample is taken

### WHEN TO USE IT
Use equation (5.44) when designing \(H[z]\) to realize an analog LTIC system by **impulse invariance**.

### EXAM TRIGGER
"Given \(h_a(t)\), find \(h[n]\)" — apply this criterion directly.

### COMMON MISUSE
Writing \(h[n] = h_a(nT)\) and forgetting the multiplier \(T\). This is the single most common error on exam problems involving impulse invariance.

> **Example:** If \(h_a(t) = e^{-2t}u(t)\), then the criterion gives:
> $$h[n] = T\, e^{-2nT}\, u[n]$$

$$h[n] = T\, h_a(nT)$$

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Equivalence condition: \(y[n] = y(nT)\) — the digital output sample must match the analog system output at each sample instant \(t = nT\).
- Analog-sampled convolution (5.42): \(y(nT) = T\sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x(mT)\, h_a[(n-m)T]\) — note the leading factor \(T\).
- Digital convolution (5.43): \(y[n] = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\, h[n-m]\) — no leading \(T\); this is the native discrete-time sum.
- Impulse invariance criterion (5.44): \(h[n] = T\, h_a(nT)\) — sample the analog impulse response and multiply by \(T\); omitting \(T\) is the classic exam trap.

*Next, use this criterion to connect analog transfer functions and digital transfer functions.*
